cwe: CWE-1385 - Missing Origin Validation in WebSockets

threat.title: WebSocket Origin Validation Bypass (Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking)
threat.description: Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking (CSWSH) occurs when WebSocket servers fail to validate the Origin header during the handshake process. When combined with cookie-based authentication and SameSite=None cookies, attackers can establish unauthorized WebSocket connections through victim browsers by hosting malicious pages. This allows complete hijacking of real-time communication channels, enabling attackers to send arbitrary messages, exfiltrate sensitive data, and perform privileged actions on behalf of authenticated users.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies target application using WebSocket with cookie-based authentication
2. Attacker discovers WebSocket endpoint lacks Origin header validation (SameSite=None cookies)
3. Attacker creates malicious HTML page with WebSocket client code targeting victim's session
4. Victim visits attacker's page while authenticated to legitimate application
5. Malicious JavaScript establishes WebSocket connection using victim's cookies
6. Attacker gains full control over WebSocket channel: reads messages, sends commands, exfiltrates data, performs admin actions (e.g., account deletion, privilege escalation)
prerequisites: WebSocket server does not validate Origin header during handshake
Cookies have SameSite=None attribute or no SameSite attribute
Victim is authenticated and visits attacker-controlled website
No CSRF token or additional authentication required for WebSocket handshake

cve_references.description: Fortinet FortiOS/FortiProxy authentication bypass via Node.js WebSocket module allowing super-admin privileges through crafted requests
MeshCentral Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking vulnerability allowing unauthorized access to remote management sessions
Cisco IOS XE Software web UI Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking leading to memory corruption and DoS
Rancher 2 through 2.2.4 Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking allowing cluster access with victim permissions

real_world_breaches.impact: 50,000+ internet-facing devices vulnerable; actively exploited in the wild; unauthorized admin account creation and configuration changes
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Authentication bypass via Node.js WebSocket module in FortiOS allowing super-admin privileges

real_world_breaches.impact: Full account takeover and complete workspace compromise; attackers could execute arbitrary code in development environments
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Gitpod's JSONRPC API used WebSockets with cookie-based authentication but lacked Origin validation

real_world_breaches.impact: Arbitrary API calls including account deletion operations via hijacked WebSocket; complete API abuse capability
real_world_breaches.root_cause: GraphQL API accessible via WebSocket with SameSite=None cookies and no Origin validation

real_world_breaches.impact: Unauthorized access to remote desktop sessions and system management interfaces
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking in MeshCentral's remote management protocol

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Lack of Origin check leads to Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking - WebSocket server provided successful 101 protocol handshake even with malicious origins
Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking allowing admin to modify shared documents from low-privilege reader account
CSWSH vulnerability enabling full account takeover via hijacked WebSocket connections
WebSocket traffic not validating Origin header, allowing cross-site hijacking attacks

security_research.title: Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking Exploitation in 2025
security_research.key_finding: Firefox Total Cookie Protection blocks cookie-based CSWSH, but Chrome and Chromium derivatives remain vulnerable. CSWSH remains widespread in production applications.

security_research.title: OWASP WebSocket Security Cheat Sheet
security_research.key_finding: WebSocket connections are not restricted by Same-Origin Policy, making Origin validation critical for security

security_research.title: Can't Stop, Won't Stop Hijacking WebSockets
security_research.key_finding: CSWSH attacks are trivial to execute when Origin validation is missing, with attack complexity rated as 'Low'

security_research.title: WebSocket Chaos: The Real-Time Protocol That's Really Insecure
security_research.key_finding: Missing Origin validation assigned CWE-1385 classification; identified as critical vulnerability pattern

mitigations.title: Implement Strict Origin Header Validation (Socket.IO + Express.js)
mitigations.description: Validate the Origin header during WebSocket handshake to ensure connections only originate from trusted domains. This is the primary defense against CSWSH attacks. Configure Socket.IO with explicit CORS allowlist and reject connections from unauthorized origins.
nist_csf: PR.PT-4 - Communications and control networks are protected
validation_steps: Test WebSocket connection from allowed origin (should succeed)
Test WebSocket connection from unauthorized origin (should fail with CORS error)
Test connection with null origin (should fail)
Test connection with wildcard origin attack (should fail)
Verify CSRF token validation in handshake middleware
Check server logs for rejected connection attempts

mitigations.title: Token-Based Authentication for WebSocket Handshake (Spring Boot)
mitigations.description: Replace cookie-based authentication with token-based authentication passed in WebSocket handshake headers or query parameters. This eliminates automatic credential inclusion by browsers, preventing CSWSH attacks even if Origin validation fails.
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are managed
PR.AC-7 - Users, devices, and other assets are authenticated
validation_steps: Test WebSocket connection without Authorization header (should fail)
Test connection with invalid JWT token (should fail)
Test connection with expired JWT token (should fail)
Test connection from unauthorized origin with valid token (should fail)
Test successful connection with valid token and allowed origin
Verify no cookies are used for WebSocket authentication

mitigations.title: Set Strict SameSite Cookie Attributes (Flask-SocketIO)
mitigations.description: Configure session cookies with SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax attributes to prevent browsers from sending cookies in cross-site WebSocket requests. This provides defense-in-depth even if Origin validation is misconfigured.

code_example.code:
from flask import Flask, session, request
from flask_socketio import SocketIO, emit, disconnect
from flask_session import Session
import jwt
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import os

app = Flask(__name__)

# Secure session configuration with strict SameSite
app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = os.environ.get('FLASK_SECRET_KEY')
app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE'] = True  # HTTPS only
app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY'] = True
app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE'] = 'Strict'  # Prevent cross-site cookie sending
app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_NAME'] = '__Secure-session'  # Secure cookie naming
app.config['PERMANENT_SESSION_LIFETIME'] = timedelta(hours=1)

# Initialize Flask-SocketIO with strict CORS
ALLOWED_ORIGINS = [
    'https://app.example.com',
    'https://admin.example.com'
]

socketio = SocketIO(
    app,
    cors_allowed_origins=ALLOWED_ORIGINS,
    logger=True,
    engineio_logger=True,
    ping_timeout=10,
    ping_interval=5
)

JWT_SECRET = os.environ.get('JWT_SECRET_KEY')

def verify_token(token):
    """Verify JWT token and return payload."""
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(token, JWT_SECRET, algorithms=['HS256'])
        return payload
    except jwt.ExpiredSignatureError:
        return None
    except jwt.InvalidTokenError:
        return None

@socketio.on('connect')
def handle_connect(auth):
    """Handle WebSocket connection with Origin and token validation."""
    # Validate Origin header
    origin = request.headers.get('Origin')
    if origin not in ALLOWED_ORIGINS:
        print(f'[SECURITY] Rejected connection from unauthorized origin: {origin}')
        disconnect()
        return False
    
    # Validate authentication token (not relying on cookies)
    if not auth or 'token' not in auth:
        print('[SECURITY] Connection rejected: No authentication token')
        disconnect()
        return False
    
    token = auth['token']
    payload = verify_token(token)
    
    if not payload:
        print('[SECURITY] Connection rejected: Invalid or expired token')
        disconnect()
        return False
    
    # Store user info in session
    session['user_id'] = payload['user_id']
    session['username'] = payload['username']
    session.permanent = True
    
    print(f'[INFO] User {payload["username"]} connected from {origin}')
    emit('connected', {'status': 'success', 'user': payload['username']})
    return True

@socketio.on('message')
def handle_message(data):
    """Handle incoming WebSocket messages."""
    if 'user_id' not in session:
        print('[SECURITY] Unauthorized message attempt')
        disconnect()
        return
    
    user_id = session['user_id']
    print(f'[MESSAGE] From user {user_id}: {data}')
    
    # Process message with validated user context
    emit('response', {
        'status': 'received',
        'timestamp': datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        'echo': data
    })

@socketio.on('disconnect')
def handle_disconnect():
    """Handle WebSocket disconnection."""
    user_id = session.get('user_id', 'unknown')
    print(f'[INFO] User {user_id} disconnected')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Run with TLS in production
    socketio.run(
        app,
        host='0.0.0.0',
        port=5000,
        ssl_context=('cert.pem', 'key.pem'),  # TLS certificates
        debug=False
    )

nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Verify session cookie has SameSite=Strict in browser DevTools
Test cross-site WebSocket connection (should fail due to no cookies sent)
Verify cookie is only sent over HTTPS (Secure flag)
Confirm cookie is not accessible via JavaScript (HttpOnly flag)
Test token-based authentication flow
Verify Origin validation rejects unauthorized domains

mitigations.title: Implement CSRF Token Validation for WebSocket Handshake
mitigations.description: Add CSRF token validation to WebSocket handshake requests, similar to form submissions. Generate unique tokens per user session and validate them before establishing WebSocket connections. This provides an additional layer of defense against CSWSH attacks.
nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Test WebSocket connection without CSRF token (should fail)
Test connection with invalid CSRF token (should fail)
Test connection with expired CSRF token (should fail)
Test successful connection with valid fresh CSRF token
Verify token is single-use (reuse should fail)
Confirm token cleanup runs periodically

indicators: WebSocket connections from unexpected geographic locations or IP ranges
Multiple WebSocket connections from single user with different origins
WebSocket handshake requests with null or mismatched Origin headers
Rapid succession of failed WebSocket connection attempts
WebSocket traffic to sensitive endpoints without proper authentication headers
Unusual patterns in WebSocket message frequency or content
WebSocket connections established outside normal business hours for service accounts

log_queries.query:
// Detect WebSocket connections from unauthorized origins
let AllowedOrigins = dynamic(['https://app.example.com', 'https://admin.example.com']);
W3CIISLog
| where csMethod == 'GET' and csUriStem contains '/socket.io/'
| where scStatus == 101  // WebSocket upgrade successful
| extend Origin = extract(@'Origin:\s*([^\r\n]+)', 1, csReferer)
| where Origin !in (AllowedOrigins) and isnotempty(Origin)
| summarize 
    ConnectionAttempts = count(),
    UniqueIPs = dcount(cIP),
    Origins = make_set(Origin)
    by bin(TimeGenerated, 5m), csUsername
| where ConnectionAttempts > 3
| project TimeGenerated, csUsername, ConnectionAttempts, UniqueIPs, Origins
| order by ConnectionAttempts desc

// Detect WebSocket handshake with null or missing Origin header
W3CIISLog
| where csMethod == 'GET' and scStatus == 101
| where csUriStem has_any ('/socket.io/', '/ws', '/websocket')
| extend Origin = extract(@'Origin:\s*([^\r\n]+)', 1, csReferer)
| where Origin == 'null' or isempty(Origin)
| project 
    TimeGenerated,
    ClientIP = cIP,
    UserAgent = csUserAgent,
    Origin,
    URI = csUriStem,
    User = csUsername
| summarize Count = count() by ClientIP, bin(TimeGenerated, 1h)
| where Count > 5

index=web_logs sourcetype=access_combined
| search status=101 uri_path="*/socket.io/*" OR uri_path="*/ws" OR uri_path="*/websocket"
| eval allowed_origins="https://app.example.com,https://admin.example.com"
| where NOT match(origin, "^(https://app\.example\.com|https://admin\.example\.com)$")
| stats count AS connection_attempts, dc(clientip) AS unique_ips, values(origin) AS origins by _time span=5m, username
| where connection_attempts > 3
| sort - connection_attempts
| table _time, username, connection_attempts, unique_ips, origins

http.response.status_code: 101 AND (
  url.path: */socket.io/* OR 
  url.path: */ws OR 
  url.path: */websocket
) AND NOT http.request.headers.origin: (
  "https://app.example.com" OR 
  "https://admin.example.com"
)

fields @timestamp, @message, origin, clientIp, statusCode, uri
| filter statusCode = 101
| filter uri like /\/socket\.io\//
| filter origin != 'https://app.example.com' and origin != 'https://admin.example.com'
| stats count() as connectionAttempts, count_distinct(clientIp) as uniqueIPs by bin(5m) as time_window, userId
| filter connectionAttempts > 3
| sort connectionAttempts desc

index=app_logs sourcetype=websocket_logs
| search event_type="websocket_handshake"
| eval is_suspicious = if(
    (isnull(origin) OR origin="null" OR NOT match(origin, "^https://[a-z0-9-]+\.example\.com$")) AND
    auth_method="cookie" AND
    same_site!="Strict",
    1, 0
  )
| where is_suspicious=1
| stats 
    count AS total_attempts,
    dc(session_id) AS unique_sessions,
    values(origin) AS suspicious_origins,
    values(user_agent) AS user_agents
    by clientip, user_id
| where total_attempts > 2
| eval risk_score = case(
    total_attempts > 10, "critical",
    total_attempts > 5, "high",
    total_attempts > 2, "medium",
    1=1, "low"
  )
| table clientip, user_id, total_attempts, unique_sessions, suspicious_origins, risk_score
